prompt_md: Which smart pointer should you choose by default when you have a single, clear owner of a heap resource?

options.a: std::shared_ptr, because it is safer due to reference counting
options.b: std::unique_ptr, because it expresses single ownership with zero overhead
options.c: std::weak_ptr, because it avoids reference cycles
options.d: A raw pointer, because smart pointers add runtime cost

explanation_md: `std::unique_ptr` is the default choice: it is the same size as a raw pointer, has no runtime overhead beyond the destructor call, and communicates single ownership clearly in the type. `shared_ptr` adds atomic reference-count operations; use it only when ownership genuinely is shared.

`a.reset()` drops the strong reference count to 0, immediately destroying R and printing "gone". `w` is a weak observer — it did not prevent destruction. `w.lock()` returns null, so the else branch prints "dead".

prompt_md: Two nodes each hold a `shared_ptr` to the other. What is the consequence?

options.a: The program crashes with a stack overflow
options.b: Both nodes leak — their reference counts never reach zero
options.c: The compiler detects the cycle and refuses to compile
options.d: Only the second node leaks; the first is properly freed

explanation_md: Each node's reference count stays at at least 1 because the other node holds it. When the local variables go out of scope, the counts drop from 2 to 1, not to 0. Neither destructor runs. This is a classic reference cycle. The fix is to make one of the back-pointers a `weak_ptr`.

prompt_md: What is the rule of five about?

options.a: You must always define exactly five constructors in every class
options.b: If you define any of destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment, move constructor, or move assignment, you should explicitly define (or delete) all five
options.c: A class must not exceed five data members for move semantics to work
options.d: Move constructors are only generated when a class has five or fewer members

explanation_md: Defining any one of the five special member functions signals that the class manages a resource in a non-default way. The compiler's auto-generated versions may not do the right thing for the other four. Explicitly defining or deleting all five ensures correct copy, move, and destruction behavior.

`std::move(p)` casts `p` to an rvalue reference, enabling `unique_ptr`'s move constructor to transfer ownership into `take`'s parameter. After the call, `p` is null. Inside `take`, the value 55 is printed before the function returns and the local `p` destructs.
